content: Hello Rust For Linux community. I'm currently making a filesystem (EROFS)port in rust and i want to also somehow merge this into the kernel. Since this is a total rewrite of the both kernel drivers and userspace utilities and i thought abstracting some common parts of these two into a single crate will reduce a lot of work for me. My current crate now just depends on core and i think it's capable enough to interact with the kernel. How can i import the crate into the kernel(lib.rs) without Cargo. Any suggestions or help are thoroughly appreciated thanks!

That is great to hear :) Are you planning to upstream that filesystem?

How can i import the crate into the kernel(lib.rs) without Cargo.

The easiest at the moment is probably to put your library as a module within the kernel crate.

Apart from the default kernel/core/alloc cfg provided in rust/Makefile, can i specify my own extra cfgs for rustc so that my in-kernel rust driver source can find the crate i introduce?

I am not sure what you mean by "cfg", but if you mean the build rules, yes, you can also add a new crate manually in rust/Makefile.

Are there any built-in rlib recipes so that i can designate a lib.rs and produce a rlib file and then make it to the linking stage?

Not yet, but we are working on a way to handle cases like that (though for in-tree code mostly, i.e. it is not intended to build crates from crates.io -- that will likely not be supported at any point, at least not in a way that downloads things automatically).

So maybe we need some enhancements on this part too?

Yes, that is another thing that will be changed.

The easiest at the moment is probably to put your library as a module within the kernel crate.

Yeah, this is easy. But it doesn't seem an elegant solution for me. I guess the current design of rust system is just to provide abstraction for the kernel, but if each module has its own crate deps, it should be kept in the same directory as intended for C source file.

I am not sure what you mean by "cfg", but if you mean the build rules, yes, you can also add a new crate manually in rust/Makefile.

So i'll elaborate more here to make things clear . So i have this crate called let's say erofs-sys and i want to put this into the fs/erofs directory. I'm also gonna put some extra rust source files into the directory so that i can bridge the interactions with the kernel crate, let's say super-helper.rs which provides rust implemented helper to super.c. So here What i intend to do is.
1) Configure the Kconfig to allow rust implemented source files to be compiled.
2) Configure the fs/erofs/Makefile locally to compile the erofs-sys crate into a built-in.rlib
3) Configure the fs/erofs/Makefile locally to compile the super-helper.rs by importing the erofs-sys as a dep crate to rustc.
4) Configure the cbindgen to generate the bindings to c with the given super-helper.rs
5) Modify the linking recipes so that super-helper.o and built-in.rlib will be linked to the kernel module
6) Modify the generate_rust_analyzer.py so that when rust-analyzer encounters super-helper.rs it will know the location of the erofs-sys and thus correctly provides lsp services.
So i'm stuck on 3). 2) seems possible though by manually creating custom rules in KBuild. in 3), i have to require rustc to use module-specific externs so that Make can pass correct compilation crates it needs to the rustc. I do find that in Makefile.build you guys have hardcoded the crate names. I think i cannot append module-specific externs if i want to import my custom crate to the compilation systems.

And generally speaking, i think allowing third-party core/alloc crates is necessary for rust-for-linux as a whole. For instance, if future cryptography developers wants to introduce a new algorithm which implemented in rust with memory-safety guarantees, kernel can use it right away with just some minor codes to bridge the third-party interfaces with the kernel abstraction. It will be less burdensome to maintain, i guess.
BTW, that filesystem is already stable upstream. I'm just planning to introduce an experimental rust impl :)

Yeah, this is easy. But it doesn't seem an elegant solution for me. I guess the current design of rust system is just to provide abstraction for the kernel, but if each module has its own crate deps, it should be kept in the same directory as intended for C source file.

It is not really about being "elegant" or not; it is that the current system was just the minimal approach to give Rust a try in the kernel. As mentioned, the plan is to have something more involved.

So i'll elaborate more here to make things clear

All that you mention should be possible, but if you intend to upstream that, then it needs to be agreed upon, which is why my first question was: is this intended to go upstream?

And generally speaking, i think allowing third-party core/alloc crates is necessary for rust-for-linux as a whole. For instance, if future cryptography developers wants to introduce a new algorithm which implemented in rust with memory-safety guarantees, kernel can use it right away with just some minor codes to bridge the third-party interfaces with the kernel abstraction. It will be less burdensome to maintain, i guess.

We are aware, but there are several constraints. Please see https://rust-for-linux.com/third-party-crates.

BTW, that filesystem is already stable upstream. I'm just planning to introduce an experimental rust impl :)

I was referring to your Rust code, for the reasons stated above. Does "planning to introduce" mean upstreaming? If yes, are upstream maintainers OK with having a duplicate implementation? (The usual rule is that there cannot be duplicate implementations, which is why I am asking)

Thanks!

Alright, sorry for late reply. We are planning to upstream that right now, upstream contributors did agree to make those experimental rust implementation as an optional replacement with the default C implementation before the virtual file system abstraction is mature enough to host the rust-only driver . Avoiding replying on crate systemis ok for me so far, as i can put my crate source codes and use them as mods and things can compile. Anyway, huge thanks for your caution and replies though.
